topic: Two strangers realize they've been dating the same person
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee did little to soothe the knot tightening in Elias's chest. He stared at the woman across the small cafe table, a familiar, infuriating curve to her smile playing on her lips. It was the same tilt of her head, the way she tucked a stray strand of dark hair behind her ear—details that had been etched into his memory over the last three weeks. He took a slow, deliberate sip, the bitterness sharp on his tongue. *This is impossible,* he thought, the realization a dull ache behind his eyes. He watched her laugh at something the barista said, and a muscle jumped in his jaw. He needed to leave, needed distance, but the sheer, undeniable familiarity of her presence kept him rooted to the chair.

[story 2]

"You look like you've seen a ghost," Clara remarked, leaning back in her chair with a sigh that sounded practiced. Leo felt a sudden, intense heat rising in his neck. He adjusted his collar, a nervous habit he hadn't realized he was indulging. He glanced over, meeting her eyes, and the world seemed to tilt on its axis. The precise shade of hazel in her irises, the slight crinkle at the corners when she concentrated—it was all there. He cleared his throat, the sound surprisingly loud in the quiet space. "Just… a long day," he managed, his voice a little too tight. He wanted to stand up, to bolt out the door, but his feet felt strangely weighted, stuck to the floor by this unwelcome recognition.

[story 3]

Maya tapped her pen against the notepad, the rhythmic sound a desperate attempt to anchor herself. She kept catching herself looking at the man across the crowded room. He was sketching intently in a worn leather-bound book, his brow furrowed in concentration. Every time he shifted his weight, the familiar set of his jaw, the way he chewed on the end of his pen—it was an unwelcome, heavy weight settling in her stomach. She picked up her water glass, the ice clinking too loudly. *No, this can't be happening.* She forced herself to look away, focusing instead on the pattern of the worn carpet, trying to physically disengage from the undeniable pull of shared history.
